4 min read
Just now

--

Why the greatest journey may not be from one city to another, but from one story to another.
For generations, when we have spoken about mobility, we have usually meant one thing: movement.
The movement of people.
The movement of goods.
The movement of vehicles.
Having spent more than three decades working in the mobility sector — as a business executive, researcher and, more recently, through doctoral research on the future of mobility — I have come to appreciate how profoundly mobility shapes economic opportunity. Roads connect markets. Railways connect industries. Airports connect nations. Better mobility creates access, productivity and prosperity.
But over time I began to notice something curious.
Even where physical mobility improves, many people remain stuck.
Not because they cannot travel.
Because they cannot imagine themselves moving.
A young graduate can travel from Kampala to Nairobi, from Nairobi to London, or from London to New York. Yet mentally, they may remain imprisoned by a single sentence:
“I studied sociology.”
“I am an engineer.”
“I am just a teacher.”
“I own a petrol station.”
These are not simply descriptions.
They are narratives.
And narratives have extraordinary power.
They shape what opportunities we notice, what risks we are willing to take and, perhaps most importantly, what possibilities we dismiss before we have even explored them.
This has led me to what I believe is an important observation:
Before people experience economic mobility, they often experience narrative mobility.
I define narrative mobility as the movement from one story about oneself to another.
It is the moment when a nurse stops saying,
“I belong in a hospital,”
and begins asking,
“Where else could my ability to care for people create value?”
It is the moment when a mathematics graduate stops saying,
“I am not an accountant,”
and begins asking,
“Where else is my analytical thinking valuable?”
It is the moment when a restaurant owner stops saying,
“I am in the restaurant business,”
and begins asking,
“What capabilities have I built that could flourish in completely different industries?”
The external journey almost always begins with an internal one.
Beyond Reskilling
Today, much of the global conversation is about reskilling.
The assumption is understandable: the world is changing, therefore people need new skills.
There is truth in that.
But I wonder whether we have overlooked another question.
Before asking what else people should learn, have we helped them discover the value hidden within what they already know?
Perhaps the greatest untapped resource in our economies is not undiscovered knowledge.
Perhaps it is the hidden economic potential of knowledge and experience that already exist.
The Hidden Crisis of Educated Adult Children
This question becomes particularly important when we consider one of the most worrying developments across East Africa.
Thousands of educated adult children possess degrees, master’s qualifications and even doctorates, yet remain unable to translate years of education into meaningful economic traction.
The conventional explanation is that there are too few jobs.
Another is that their qualifications no longer match labour market demand.
Both explanations contain truth.
But I believe there is a third explanation that deserves greater attention.
Many educated adults remain trapped inside the narrative created by their qualification.
They continue asking,
“What job can I get with my degree?”
instead of asking,
“What capabilities have I developed, and in how many different ways could those capabilities create value?”
That is not merely a career question.
It is a mobility question.
Not physical mobility.
Narrative mobility.
The inability to move beyond one professional identity can become just as limiting as the inability to travel beyond one’s village.
The Story Must Move First
Once the narrative changes, something remarkable often happens.
Capabilities that were previously invisible become visible.
New industries suddenly become relevant.
Entrepreneurial opportunities appear.
Unexpected collaborations emerge.
The individual has not acquired new knowledge overnight.
What has changed is the story through which they interpret their existing knowledge.
And when the story changes, action often follows.
The Knowledge-to-Value Initiative
This insight lies at the heart of what I call the Knowledge-to-Value Initiative.
Its purpose is not simply to help educated adult children find employment.
Its purpose is to help people recognise, activate and express the capabilities they have already spent years developing.
Employment may be one outcome.
Entrepreneurship may be another.
Consulting, leadership, innovation, public service or entirely new industries may be others.
The goal is not simply to find work.
The goal is to unlock value.
A Different Kind of Mobility
Perhaps mobility is broader than we have imagined.
There is physical mobility, which moves people.
There is economic mobility, which changes lives.
And there is narrative mobility, which makes both possible.
For perhaps the greatest journey any educated person will ever undertake is not the journey from one city to another.
It is the journey from the story that confines them to the story that sets them free.
Dr. Petero Wamala, DBA writes about the knowledge-action gap, narrative mobility, and practical ways of helping individuals and organisations transform existing knowledge into opportunity, impact and lasting value.
